question.text: When assessing the consistency of a mutual fund's performance, what is the most reliable approach?

answer.text: Looking at only the last 1-year return as it reflects the most current performance
Checking if the fund delivered the highest return in any single year
Evaluating the fund's performance across different time periods and market cycles (bull and bear phases)
Selecting the fund with the highest AUM as larger funds always perform better

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** Consistency of performance means the fund has delivered competitive returns across different market conditions -- both bull and bear phases. A fund that ranks in the top quartile during bull markets but drops to the bottom quartile during corrections is inconsistent. Evaluating returns across 1-year, 3-year, 5-year, and through complete market cycles gives a more comprehensive picture of the fund manager's skill.
**The Trap:** Looking at only 1-year returns can be misleading -- a fund that took excessive risk might top the charts in one year but crash the next. Similarly, the highest single-year return does not indicate consistency; it might indicate aggressive risk-taking. AUM size has no direct correlation with performance quality.
**Cross-Exam Context:** Quantitative metrics for consistency include: rolling returns (returns over overlapping periods), standard deviation (volatility), Sharpe ratio (risk-adjusted return), and information ratio (consistency of alpha over benchmark). Qualitative factors include fund manager tenure, investment process stability, and AMC pedigree.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** When presenting funds to clients, show rolling returns charts rather than point-to-point returns. A fund that consistently delivers 12-15% across most 3-year rolling periods is more reliable than one that swings between -5% and 30%. Consistency builds client confidence and reduces the temptation to switch funds frequently.
